sources.reviewedCode:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Use the queue to mirror how the tree unfolds outward from the root.
#     def __init__(self, x):
# [Reader note] State setup
# This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Queue-driven processing, level boundaries, and translating a tree shape into ordered output groups.
#         self.val = x
#         self.left = None
#         self.right = None


class Solution:
    def levelOrder(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[List[int]]:
        res = []
        q = collections.deque()
        if root:
            q.append(root)

        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while q:
            val = []

            for i in range(len(q)):
                node = q.popleft()
                val.append(node.val)
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: The important part is organizing level boundaries cleanly instead of mixing all nodes into one flat pass.
                if node.left:
                    q.append(node.left)
                if node.right:
                    q.append(node.right)
            res.append(val)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return res

sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(n)

class TreeNode(object):
    # [Reader note] Entry point
    # Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Use the queue to mirror how the tree unfolds outward from the root.
    def __init__(self, x):
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Queue-driven processing, level boundaries, and translating a tree shape into ordered output groups.
        self.val = x
        self.left = None
        self.right = None


class Solution(object):
    # @param root, a tree node
    # @return a list of lists of integers
    def levelOrder(self, root):
        if root is None:
            return []
        result, current = [], [root]
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while current:
            next_level, vals = [], []
            for node in current:
                vals.append(node.val)
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: The important part is organizing level boundaries cleanly instead of mixing all nodes into one flat pass.
                if node.left:
                    next_level.append(node.left)
                if node.right:
                    next_level.append(node.right)
            current = next_level
            result.append(vals)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return result

sources.reviewedCode:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Use the queue to mirror how the tree unfolds outward from the root.
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
# [Reader note] State setup
# This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Queue-driven processing, level boundaries, and translating a tree shape into ordered output groups.
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right
class Solution:
    def levelOrder(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> List[List[int]]:
        ans = []
        if root is None:
            return ans
        q = deque([root])
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while q:
            t = []
            for _ in range(len(q)):
                node = q.popleft()
                t.append(node.val)
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: The important part is organizing level boundaries cleanly instead of mixing all nodes into one flat pass.
                if node.left:
                    q.append(node.left)
                if node.right:
                    q.append(node.right)
            ans.append(t)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return ans

complexityHints: Note that the time complexity is actually O(N^2) if we consider the fact that we use an array as a queue. Calling Array.shift() takes O(N).
